stories.title: Events Operator Glasshouse Signs 30-Year Lease for 3 World Trade Center
stories.excerpt: Glasshouse has signed a long-term lease for 66,436 square feet at 3 World Trade Center. The 30-year agreement for a portion of the ground floor and the entirety of the second and third floors brings the event, hospitality and experiential venue operator to the World Trade Center campus for its fourth Manhattan location. CBRE’s Chris Mansfield, Anthony Dattoma and Zachary Weil represented Glasshouse. The transaction involved a three-party structure among Glasshouse, Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield (URW) and the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. URW represented itself. “This transaction represents one of the most significant experiential leasing transactions completed in Manhattan in recent years,” said Dattoma. “Beyond creating a flagship venue for Glasshouse, this lease complements the continued momentum and surge of leasing activity across Lower Manhattan. The new venue is expected to attract more than 250,000 visitors annually, bringing additional energy and activation to the World Trade Center campus while further strengthening Downtown as one of New York City’s premier destinations for business, hospitality, culture and community engagement.” The post Events Operator Glasshouse Signs 30-Year Lease for 3 World Trade Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Glasshouse signs 30-year lease for 66,436 SF at 3 World Trade Center, Manhattan.
Event venue expected to attract 250,000+ annual visitors to Lower Manhattan campus.
Transaction signals strong experiential/hospitality tenant demand in NYC office market.
stories.summary: Events operator Glasshouse committed to a three-decade lease spanning ground and second-third floors of 3 World Trade Center, representing one of Manhattan's largest experiential leases in years. The venue expects to draw over 250,000 visitors annually, anchoring URW's continued momentum at the World Trade Center campus. This transaction reflects resurgent leasing activity and tenant confidence in Downtown Manhattan as a premier business and hospitality destination.
stories.impact: While not directly a multifamily play, this signals strong downtown NYC activation and foot-traffic generation that benefits surrounding residential demand. Glasshouse's massive visitor draw (250K+/year) strengthens the case for mixed-use residential development in Lower Manhattan, particularly rental apartment projects targeting young professionals drawn by nightlife and experiential amenities near transit.

stories.title: Silver Lake Development Parcel Increases Density Through Re-Entitlement
stories.excerpt: Northmarq’s Los Angeles Investment Sales team of Mike Hanassab, Steven Goldstein and Elliot Hassan arranged the $5.25-million sale of Hyperion Residences, a three-parcel development site located at 835 N. Hyperion Ave. in Los Angeles’ Silver Lake neighborhood. Northmarq represented the seller, Hyperion Apartments LP. The buyer is a locally based affordable housing developer. The site was delivered fully entitled and Ready-to-Issue for a six-story, approximately 54-unit project under Transit Oriented Communities Tier 3. During escrow, the buyer leveraged Executive Directive 1 to re-entitle the parcels for 105 units, increasing density and reducing per-unit cost significantly. The community is now under construction as a mixed-income development. “Silver Lake remains one of Los Angeles’ most entitlement-constrained corridors, and sites that pencil for Executive Directive 1 projects are increasingly sought after,” said Hanassab. “The buyer recognized the deeper opportunity and moved swiftly to re-entitle the parcels during escrow, bringing their basis to roughly $50,000 per buildable unit — an exceptional outcome for both parties.” The post Silver Lake Development Parcel Increases Density Through Re-Entitlement appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hyperion Residences, Silver Lake LA, re-entitled from 54 to 105 units via Executive Directive 1.
Buyer achieved ~$50K per buildable unit basis by leveraging density bonus post-acquisition.
Silver Lake remains entitlement-constrained; ED1 sites increasingly competitive for affordable developers.
stories.summary: A locally based affordable housing developer purchased Hyperion Residences (835 N. Hyperion Ave., Silver Lake) for $5.25M fully entitled for 54 units, then swiftly re-entitled during escrow to 105 units using Executive Directive 1 density incentives. The re-entitlement nearly doubled unit count while reducing per-unit cost to roughly $50K—a significant arbitrage on entitled land in a supply-constrained submarket. The project is now under construction as mixed-income housing.
stories.impact: Silver Lake's entitlement constraints and ED1 density bonus availability create a window for strategic land acquisition and re-entitlement plays in LA. Multifamily developers should monitor other entitled but underbuilt sites in transit-adjacent LA corridors eligible for ED1; the $50K/unit basis demonstrates the leverage available when entitlements are re-optimized post-close, improving project feasibility in high-cost urban submarkets.

stories.title: Condo High-Rise Launches Construction in Sunnyside
stories.excerpt: Construction has begun at 51-02 Roosevelt Ave., a 19-story condominium rising at the border of Sunnyside. The development is led by Hiwin Group USA, whose previous work includes 300 W. 30th St. in Chelsea, a 16-story, 69-residence condo project that sold out in nine months. Scheduled for delivery in 2029, 51-02 Roosevelt Ave. represents a rare high-rise addition to the neighborhood, according to Acre NY Realty, which is serving as sales and marketing representative.”51-02 Roosevelt Ave. is more than a new residential building. It is a meaningful addition to the Sunnyside community,” said Cathy Huang, CEO of Acre NY Realty. “With its height, visibility program and strong transit access, this project has the potential to become a true neighborhood landmark.” The project will include 84 residential units, including 70 one-bedroom and 14 two-bedroom residences. It will also provide ground-floor retail, commercial office space, community-oriented medical space, residential recreation areas, terraces, and parking access. The post Condo High-Rise Launches Construction in Sunnyside appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: 51-02 Roosevelt Ave., Sunnyside Queens: 19-story, 84-unit condo launched construction, delivery 2029.
Hiwin Group USA developer; prior Chelsea project (69 units) sold out in nine months.
Rare high-rise addition to neighborhood with strong transit; includes ground-floor retail and office.
stories.summary: Hiwin Group USA broke ground on a 19-story condominium at the Sunnyside-border with 84 units (70 one-bed, 14 two-bed), ground-floor retail and office, and community spaces. Delivery is slated for 2029. The developer's track record includes a 69-unit Chelsea condo (300 W. 30th St.) that sold out in nine months, signaling strong sponsor credibility and buyer demand for urban product in gateway markets.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily rental development; this is condo (for-sale) product. However, the Sunnyside location and strong transit access underscore robust demand for high-rise residential at Queens-Manhattan borders. Rental developers should note that sell-out velocity in transit-rich outer-borough projects validates demand; similar sites may support high-density rental apartments if zoning permits.

stories.title: Midwood Buys Mixed-Use Building Across From Former Downtown Brooklyn Macy’s
stories.excerpt: A four-story office and retail building across the street from the former Macy’s department store in Downtown Brooklyn has traded, Commercial Observer has learned. Midwood Investment & Development, a New York City-based real estate development and management firm, purchased 423 Fulton Street — opposite the former Macy’s at 422 Fulton Street — for $9.5 million, according to broker KSR. The property on the corner of Fulton and Pearl streets is home to Panda Express in the ground-floor retail space and Staywell Medical Group in the office space. The seller of 423 Fulton Street is BNN Fulton Flushing Owner, an LLC comprising several parties, including real estate investor Ralph Braha and the Nakash family, which owns Jordache Enterprises. The Nakash family also owns the historic Versace Mansion in Miami Beach, Fla., where it just secured a $44.7 million refinancing loan in May. KSR’s Albert Sultan and Joseph Ash brokered the 423 Fulton Street deal for both the buyer and the seller entity. A spokesperson for Midwood did not immediately respond to a request for comment, while a spokesperson for Jordache could not be reached for comment. While Midwood’s exact plans for its new property are unclear, the deal comes as Downtown Brooklyn experiences a bit of a retail renaissance, especially following news that the neighborhood’s former Macy’s department store will be redeveloped into a five-floor retail and amusement destination called BKX, as CO first reported. Owners United American Land and Jackson Group plan to transform the 440,000-square-foot space at 422 Fulton Street into a hot spot for large-format retail tenants, a wellness or fitness facility, and food and beverage companies. And while there’s still 125 available storefronts in Downtown Brooklyn, the area has found success in retail that meets its population’s needs, including three new supermarkets on Fulton Street, Regina Myer, president of the Downtown Brooklyn Partnership, said during CO’s 2026 Brooklyn Foru
bullets: Midwood Investment & Development acquired 423 Fulton Street, Downtown Brooklyn, for $9.5M.
Four-story mixed-use building opposite former Macy's; tenanted by Panda Express and Staywell Medical.
Downtown Brooklyn continues consolidating with mixed-use acquisitions near transit and anchors.
stories.summary: Midwood Investment & Development purchased a four-story office-retail building at 423 Fulton Street (corner of Pearl) in Downtown Brooklyn for $9.5M, directly across from the former Macy's department store. The asset is currently leased to Panda Express (ground-floor) and Staywell Medical Group (office). The transaction underscores continued real estate activity in the Downtown Brooklyn submarket despite broader retail challenges.
stories.impact: Minor relevance to multifamily. The deal signals that mixed-use sites near Downtown Brooklyn transit and landmark anchors (even vacant ones like former Macy's) attract development capital. If the former Macy's site is redeveloped for residential, it could trigger a wave of residential-heavy mixed-use conversions in the submarket, driving multifamily demand upward. Monitor the vacancy and reuse plans for the 422 Fulton Street (old Macy's) site.

stories.title: JPMorgan Funnels $205M to Refi Blackstone’s East Hotel in Miami
stories.excerpt: Blackstone Real Estate has once again landed debt from J.P. Morgan Chase to refinance a South Florida hotel. The bank supplied a $205 million loan backed by the East Miami hotel in the Brickell district, property records show. The 39-story hotel houses 352 rooms, including 89 serviced apartments, a 20,000-square-foot pool deck and the Sugar rooftop bar, located within the 5 million-square-foot Brickell City Centre mixed-use complex at 788 Brickell Plaza. New York-based private equity giant Blackstone purchased the 536,070-square-foot asset for about $300 million last year from Trinity Investments and Certares Real Estate Management. The refinancing appears to be the first loan Blackstone has loaded onto the property, which was completed in 2016. East Miami is one of three hotels in the luxury East brand. The others are in Hong Kong and Beijing. Just last month, J.P. Morgan Chase funneled $115 million to refinance Blackstone’s oceanfront W hotel in Fort Lauderdale. The firm had purchased the 346-room asset for $153 million in 2024. The financing comes on the back of other major refinancings in South Florida. In April, Trinity Investments and UBS nabbed $600 million for the 1,000-room, oceanfront Diplomat Beach Resort in Hollywood also from J.P. Morgan Chase and Citi Real Estate Funding. Representatives for Blackstone did not immediately provide comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: JPMorgan supplied $205M refi loan for Blackstone's East Miami hotel in Brickell.
39-story asset: 352 rooms, 89 serviced apartments, 20K SF pool deck in 5M SF mixed-use complex.
Blackstone acquired for ~$300M in 2023; first debt layered on property; strong lender appetite for premium hospitality.
stories.summary: JPMorgan Chase provided a $205M refinancing loan for Blackstone Real Estate's East Miami hotel, a 39-story luxury property within the 5M-square-foot Brickell City Centre. The asset contains 352 rooms (including 89 serviced apartments), premium amenities, and was acquired by Blackstone for ~$300M in 2023 from Trinity/Certares. This is the first debt placed on the property since completion in 2016, signaling strong lender confidence in premium South Florida hospitality assets.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact. However, strong refinancing appetite for luxury hotels in major mixed-use complexes (Brickell City Centre) suggests robust capital availability and lender confidence in mixed-use submarkets. This supports the feasibility of large residential-hotel hybrid developments in similar South Florida submarkets; lenders viewing hotel components as stabilizing anchors may be more receptive to mixed-use residential financing.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Closes Sale of North Shore Industrial Condo
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of an industrial condominium at 30 Log Bridge Rd. in Middleton, MA. The asset sold for $2,525,000. “The quality of this industrial condo drove interest from owner-users and investors,” said Mattias Edenkrans, associate director investments in Marcus & Millichap’s Boston office. “Ultimately, the low market vacancy in this core North Shore market appealed to an investor group that purchased with conviction, allowing us to achieve certainty of execution for our client.” Edenkrans and Luigi Lessa of Marcus & Millichap marketed the property on behalf of the seller, E Homes Realty, LLC, and procured the buyer, Richardson Green. The property, located at 30 Log Bridge Road #303, is a 12,975-square-foot industrial condominium on a 13.51-acre lot. The property is split into a 7,025-square-foot warehouse, a 2,975-square-foot showroom on the first floor and a 2,975-square-foot second-floor office space. It was built in 2016 and includes 25 parking spaces. The post Marcus & Millichap Closes Sale of North Shore Industrial Condo appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap closed $2.525M sale of 12,975 SF industrial condo in Middleton, MA (North Shore).
Low market vacancy in North Shore drew investor conviction despite industrial headwinds.
Buyer: Richardson Green; 2016-built, mixed warehouse-showroom-office condo with 25 parking spaces.
stories.summary: An industrial condominium at 30 Log Bridge Road, Middleton, MA sold for $2.525M, comprising 7,025 SF warehouse, 2,975 SF showroom, and 2,975 SF office space on a 13.51-acre lot. Built in 2016, the property features 25 parking spaces. Low market vacancy in the core North Shore market attracted investor conviction despite broader industrial headwinds, driving certainty of execution for the seller.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily relevance. Low vacancy in North Shore industrial underscores tight supply in the Boston metro's secondary markets, which may indicate broader supply constraints lifting rents across submarkets and supporting multifamily asset valuations and development feasibility in the region.

stories.title: CBRE Adds Freddie Mac’s Robert Koontz to Lead Multifamily Debt Capital Markets
stories.excerpt: CBRE has added Robert Koontz as head of multifamily debt capital markets after he led a similar role at Freddie Mac, the company announced Monday. Koontz, who spent 17 years at Freddie Mac and most recently served as head of multifamily capital markets at the government-sponsored enterprise, joined CBRE on July 6 as part of the brokerage’s efforts to expand its multifamily loan business. He will report to James Millon, co-head of capital markets, and work closely with Kelli Carhart, head of multifamily capital markets who worked with Koontz at Freddie Mac prior to joining CBRE in 2022. Koontz left Freddie Mac in November 2025. “Robert was a free agent and is super talented, and he’s one of those people that if they are available you figure out a way to bring them forward,” Carhart told Commercial Observer. “We’re bringing on somebody who is a platform builder, who culturally is a great fit, a great leader and a great visionary.’ At Freddie Mac, Koontz built out the GSE’s K-Deal CMBS program that launched in 2009. He will be focused at CBRE on expanding its balance sheet and risk transfer strategies which includes more bridge debt offerings such as bridge-to-agency loans. Koontz, who will split his time between Washington. D.C.. and New York, arrives at CBRE in a growth period for multifamily debt coming off a more than $30 billion origination output in 2025 with this year’s volume up 60 percent, according to Carhart. “The combination of origination capabilities, market intelligence, and an integrated platform across commercial real estate sales and investment banking creates a clear competitive advantage for clients,” Koontz said in a statement. “I look forward to working with this team to drive execution and expand what we can deliver for multifamily borrowers and investors across the country.” Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: CBRE hired Robert Koontz (17 years at Freddie Mac) as head of multifamily debt capital markets.
Koontz built Freddie Mac's K-Deal CMBS program (2009); will expand bridge, agency, risk-transfer at CBRE.
Senior hire signals CBRE's strategic push to capture multifamily debt origination and syndication.
stories.summary: CBRE appointed Robert Koontz, a 17-year Freddie Mac veteran and architect of the GSE's K-Deal CMBS program, as head of multifamily debt capital markets. Koontz will report to co-head James Millon and work alongside Kelli Carhart (his former Freddie Mac colleague) to expand CBRE's balance sheet, bridge-to-agency loans, and risk-transfer strategies. The hire reflects CBRE's commitment to deepen its multifamily lending business and compete aggressively for originations.
stories.impact: Capital signal: CBRE's multimillion-dollar bet on Freddie Mac talent suggests confidence in sustained multifamily debt demand and potential gaps in GSE/lender capacity to fund the pipeline. Developers should expect CBRE to become a more aggressive originator of construction and bridge debt; this may tighten pricing competition and expand available debt structures (e.g., bridge-to-agency) for qualified sponsors, easing refinance paths and potentially lowering cost of capital for new starts.

stories.title: Rubicon Point Acquires Three-Building Office Complex Near Apple Headquarters
stories.excerpt: Rubicon Point Partners (RPP) has acquired Wolfe Square, a Class A, 117,795-square-foot multi-tenant office and medical campus located adjacent to Apple’s global headquarters in Cupertino. Although the buyer didn’t disclose the acquisition price, the Silicon Valley Business Journal recently reported it as more than $50 million. “Wolfe Square represents exactly the type of quality investment in a quality location that defines our investment strategy,” said Ani Vartanian, RPP co-Founder & managing director. “This is a community-critical building with institutional-grade tenancy, a proven rent roll, and a location that simply cannot be replicated. We are proud to steward this asset for years to come.” RPP plans to bring its UnCommon hospitality platform to the property. Will Connors, Dan Renz & Michael Manas of JLL represented the seller in the transaction. Bob Simpson and Erik Hallgrimson of Cushman & Wakefield serve as the leasing brokers for the property. The post Rubicon Point Acquires Three-Building Office Complex Near Apple Headquarters appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Rubicon Point Partners acquired Wolfe Square: 117,795 SF Class A office near Apple HQ, Cupertino, >$50M.
Multi-tenant office-medical campus; RPP plans UnCommon hospitality platform activation.
Silicon Valley institutional office with proven rent roll and unreplicable location near tech anchor.
stories.summary: Rubicon Point Partners purchased Wolfe Square, a 117,795-square-foot Class A office-medical campus adjacent to Apple's global headquarters in Cupertino, for more than $50M (per Silicon Valley Business Journal). The multi-tenant property boasts institutional-grade tenancy and a proven rent roll. RPP plans to activate the asset with its UnCommon hospitality platform, leveraging proximity to one of Silicon Valley's largest employment centers.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily play, but Apple HQ proximity and RPP's hospitality overlay signal strong commercial viability in Cupertino. Office-to-residential conversions or ground-floor lifestyle retail within 1-mile of Apple's campus could support premium rental apartment demand from Apple workers and visiting executives. Monitor Cupertino zoning flexibility for office-to-residential conversions, particularly if tech employment remains robust and housing supply stays constrained.

stories.title: Fashion Platform Orly Inks 20K-SF Office Lease at 20 West 33rd Street
stories.excerpt: Consumer products and fashion platform Orly has leased 20,014 square feet of office space at 20 West 33rd Street in Midtown South, according to the landlord broker. The deal, first reported by Traded, was signed with landlord JLA Home two weeks ago. Asking rent for the 10-year lease was $55 per square foot. Orly, founded in 1980 and also known as Orly-Corp., currently manages a portfolio of over 20 brands, ranging from footwear and apparel to household products and travel accessories. Its partnerships include Aeropostale, Lucky Brand and Nine West. Orly expects to move into its new, larger space early next year, according to a post on the company’s LinkedIn page. The company’s website locates its current headquarters one block north of their new lease, at 15 West 34th Street. Representatives of Orly could not immediately be reached for comment. LSL Advisors’ Wayne Siegel, Daniel Lolai and Zach Sarraf represented the tenant in the negotiations, while Lincoln Property Company’s Jeffrey Rosenblatt and Christina De Jesus worked on behalf of JLA. An entity of JLA, a home furnishing company, purchased its office condos at 20 West 33rd Street in 2022. Other tenants at the 13-story property include home and houseware company Vanderbilt Home Collections, restaurant Turntable Jazz Chicken and painting services provider JB Painters. Spokespeople for LSL Advisors and JLA Home did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Fashion platform Orly leased 20,014 SF at 20 West 33rd Street, Midtown South, for 10 years at $55/SF.
Orly (1980 founder) manages 20+ brands including Aeropostale, Lucky Brand, Nine West; move-in early 2025.
Midtown South office leasing sustained; tenant growth supports retail-office mixed-use demand.
stories.summary: Orly, a consumer products and fashion platform managing 20+ brands (Aeropostale, Lucky Brand, Nine West), signed a 10-year lease for 20,014 square feet at 20 West 33rd Street in Midtown South at $55/SF. The company is relocating from nearby 15 West 34th Street and expects to occupy the new space in early 2025. The deal, brokered by Lincoln Property and LSL Advisors, underscores sustained office demand in the Midtown South submarket.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. Midtown South office leasing activity (especially at mid-market rents like $55/SF) suggests market health and tenancy stability. Sustained office demand in the submarket supports mixed-use residential projects; developers should continue monitoring office conversion feasibility for underutilized Manhattan office buildings, particularly in Midtown South, if office-to-residential regulatory paths open further.

stories.title: Hackman’s Television City Heads Toward Sale as Studio Distress Grips Hollywood
stories.excerpt: Television City, a landmark studio in Los Angeles that has been home to productions ranging from “The Price Is Right” to “American Idol,” is poised to hit the market in another blow to owner and studio mogul Hackman Capital Partners (HCP). A lender group led by Deutsche Bank filed a notice of default last month alleging Hackman owes more than $357 million on the 25-acre property in the Fairfax District, according to the Los Angeles Times. The default follows months of negotiations between Hackman and its lenders as the company weighs alternatives, and it’s another marquee studio property in HCP’s industry-leading portfolio to fall into distress. “As an owner of studio assets in multiple U.S. and international markets, we have seen firsthand how Los Angeles studio real estate faces significant pressure from high production costs and uncompetitive tax incentives,” HCP said in a statement shared with Commercial Observer. “Television City is a local treasure, home to decades of iconic productions, and we remain in active discussions with our lending partners as we carefully evaluate all alternatives available for its future.” It’s also the latest chapter in the unraveling of the industry’s bet that streaming would fuel an expansion and an extension of studio real estate demand. Culver City-based HCP over the past decade assembled the largest portfolio of studio properties, including a 2019 deal for $750 million to acquire Television City with its partner Affinius Capital (formerly Square Mile Capital) from CBS. HCP also planned a roughly $1 billion redevelopment that would add nearly 1 million square feet of offices, soundstages, production facilities and retail. But show business, and HCP’s portfolio, were significantly hamstrung by the post-pandemic slowdown, the 2023 writers and actors strikes, rising interest rates and studio consolidation. Earlier this year, Goldman Sachs took control of the 1.2 million-square-foot Radford Studio Center — where HCP also planned a $
bullets: Television City, Los Angeles, default notice filed by Deutsche Bank lender group; $357M+ owed.
Hackman Capital Partners studio asset in distress amid high production costs, low tax competitiveness.
Landmark 25-acre Fairfax District studio property heading toward forced sale.
stories.summary: Hackman Capital Partners' iconic Television City (25 acres, Fairfax District) received a notice of default from a Deutsche Bank-led lender group alleging over $357M in debt. The distress follows months of negotiations as HCP faces mounting pressure from elevated LA production costs and diminished tax incentives relative to other studios. The property, home to "The Price Is Right" and "American Idol," is poised to be marketed following the default.
stories.impact: Significant opportunity: Television City's 25-acre Fairfax location could become available for mixed-use redevelopment (residential, retail, office) if HCP's debt restructuring or sale results in operational exit. The site's size, proximity to West Hollywood and mid-city transit, and entertainment-district cache make it a potential trophy redevelopment for a multifamily-focused developer. Monitor the default timeline and lender intent; a 25-acre studio lot reprieve in urban LA could unlock 500–1,500+ unit residential opportunity if zoning permits mixed-use infill.

stories.title: Judge tosses suit over Two Roads’ Four Seasons Bahamas. Not so fast, plaintiff says
stories.excerpt: A judge tossed a lawsuit accusing Two Roads Development of sidelining a partner from a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas –– but the fight isn’t over.  The partner that sued, Soho Development, led by Delray Beach resident and attorney Roger Stein, says the judge addressed the wrong complaint and not the latest filing. Soho Development last summer sued Two Roads, a South Florida luxury development firm, alleging it excluded Soho from the development, while continuing to use project contracts and confidential information provided by Soho with no compensation to the firm, according to the complaint […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Soho Development sues Two Roads over $300M Four Seasons condo project exclusion, Bahamas.
Two Roads developing Ocean Club: 67 units, two-bedroom to four-bedroom units plus villas, 2028 completion.
Legal dispute over breach of 2021 NDA; presales underway despite litigation; Access Industries (Len Blavatnik) backing.
stories.summary: Soho Development, led by attorney Roger Stein, sued Two Roads Development alleging exclusion from a roughly $300M Four Seasons-branded condo project on Paradise Island, Bahamas, claiming breach of a 2021 NDA and misuse of confidential information. Two Roads is developing the Ocean Club with 67 luxury units (two-bedroom to five-bedroom) in partnership with Access Real Estate (Blavatnik-backed). Presales are underway despite litigation, with construction completion expected in 2028.
stories.impact: Limited multifamily relevance; this is luxury for-sale condo product. However, the dispute underscores capital concentration and deal complexity in high-net-worth Caribbean real estate. If the project faces delays or capital reallocation due to litigation, it could signal weakness in luxury Caribbean development sentiment. Multifamily developers in Caribbean or secondary-market luxury segments should monitor partner stability and NDA/non-compete enforceability in competitive development environments.

stories.title: From the gridiron to the high-rise: Blake Poston planning $100M Uptown Dallas high-rise
stories.excerpt: A former SMU football player is developing a $100 million, 20-story high-rise just north of Uptown Dallas, around a mile from where he used to drop into coverage on Saturdays.  Blake Poston, who was a part of the Southern Methodist University Mustangs football team for three years and went to Highland Park High School, is also the founder of Cover2 Capital. Poston told the Dallas Morning News that Cover2 is aiming to begin construction on an Uptown high-rise at 5220 McKinney Avenue late next year. The building is dubbed The Savannah, and Cover2 is still in design and city development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Cover2 Capital (founder Blake Poston) planning $100M, 20-story, 100-unit luxury rental in Uptown Dallas.
The Savannah project: 1,800 SF avg unit size, three penthouse units, ground-floor retail; late-2025 groundbreak.
Cover2 portfolio: 297-unit Wheelhouse (North Richland Hills), planned Greerhouse (Anna); emerging Dallas developer.
stories.summary: Blake Poston's Cover2 Capital is developing The Savannah, a $100M, 20-story luxury apartment high-rise at 5220 McKinney Avenue (Uptown Dallas) with 100 units (one to three bedrooms) averaging 1,800 SF, plus three penthouses and ground-floor retail. Groundbreaking is targeted for late 2025. Cover2's portfolio includes the 297-unit Wheelhouse mixed-use project (North Richland Hills) and planned Greerhouse garden-style development (Anna), positioning the firm as an emerging luxury multifamily developer in the Dallas region.
stories.impact: Strong signal of rental demand and financing confidence in Dallas/Fort Worth luxury segments. Cover2's scale-up (three concurrent projects totaling 400+ units) and $100M Uptown commitment reflect lender and investor appetite for well-capitalized new sponsors. Uptown Dallas continued densification (high-rise focus vs. suburban garden) suggests structural shift toward transit-proximate urban rental. Monitor Cover2's capital sources and debt terms as indicators of multifamily financing ease for emerging sponsors in Texas.

stories.title: South Florida Dirt: A timeline of the Vacchi vs. Stern legal battle
stories.excerpt: Developer Michael Stern teamed up with Italian investor and TikToker Gianluca Vacchi in 2024.  At the time, Stern said that Vacchi “understands new media and how to cut through the noise on social media in a way most real estate people don’t,” calling him an effective marketer and tastemaker. Online to his 22.1 million Instagram followers and 22.3 million TikTok followers, Vacchi has showcased his jetsetting lifestyle, workouts, cold plunges, and maybe most frequently, his dancing.  Two years ago, Stern and Vacchi said they were 50-50 partners on a $4 billion pipeline of JDS Development’s projects (Stern’s firm). That included […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Michael Stern (JDS Development) and Gianluca Vacchi (22M+ social followers) partnership imploded; Vacchi sues for fraud.
Vacchi claims Stern diverted his investment between projects (Mercedes-Benz, Dolce & Gabbana, 1250 West Ave, Miami).
JDS exiting 1250 West Avenue; Mercedes-Benz and D&G projects in litigation; Stern securing new capital/partners.
stories.summary: Developer Michael Stern and Italian influencer Gianluca Vacchi's 2024 partnership and 50-50 equity deal on a $4B pipeline of JDS Development projects has collapsed. Vacchi is suing Stern alleging fraud and Ponzi-scheme conduct, claiming Stern misrepresented project status and diverted capital between developments including Mercedes-Benz Places Miami, 888 Brickell (Dolce & Gabbana), and 1250 West Avenue (Miami Beach). JDS has exited 1250 West Avenue; Mercedes-Benz and D&G projects remain in litigation but Stern is pursuing new debt and partnership with developer Jeff Soffer.
stories.impact: Capital risk signal for Miami luxury condo market: high-profile developer disputes and litigation over a $4B pipeline indicate project stress, sponsor credibility concerns, and potential capital constraints. The exit from 1250 West Avenue and D&G/Mercedes-Benz entanglements suggest financing headwinds in Miami's ultra-luxury condo segment. Multifamily developers should monitor Miami condo-to-rental conversion opportunities if luxury condo projects are abandoned or stalled; tight rental supply and flight-to-quality could support apartment rents if condo development slows.

stories.title: Mark Cuban takes Adelson to court on Mavs plans for new arena
stories.excerpt: Who could’ve guessed that selling a basketball team would change who’s in charge? Apparently not Mark Cuban.  After hinting at his regret over selling the Dallas Mavericks to Las Vegas Sands on a podcast in March, the billionaire businessman isn’t just going public with his concerns about the team’s new ownership–he’s taking them to court.  Cuban, who sold his majority stake in the team to Miriam Adelson’s casino company in 2023, claims in a new court filing that he was shut out of decisions about the team’s new arena, the Dallas Morning News reported. He’s asking the court to grant […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Mark Cuban sues Miriam Adelson/Las Vegas Sands over Dallas Mavericks new arena deal and Valley View Mall site.
Cuban claims he was shut out of arena contract review despite verbal agreement to oversee team operations.
Casino gambling still conditional on Texas legalization; Mavericks arena is major Dallas development catalyst.
stories.summary: Mark Cuban, who sold his majority Mavericks stake to billionaire Miriam Adelson's Las Vegas Sands in 2023, filed a lawsuit alleging he was excluded from decisions on the team's planned new arena at Valley View Mall in Dallas. Cuban claims a verbal agreement entitled him to continue overseeing team operations while Sands pursued casino-arena development. The new arena is contingent on Texas legalizing casino gambling, a legislative effort that has stalled despite years of lobbying and campaign donations.
stories.impact: Development timing uncertainty in Dallas: the Mavericks arena at Valley View is a major mixed-use anchor catalyst for that submarket, potentially driving residential demand nearby. Cuban's lawsuit and the ongoing Texas gaming legalization impasse create ambiguity around the project's greenlight and completion timeline. Multifamily developers with nearby land or in-entitlement projects should monitor the lawsuit outcome and legislative status before committing capital; if the arena stalls or scales down, the submarket support for density weakens materially.

stories.title: Nonprofit pitches housing conversion at Palo Alto site with SB 79 proposal
stories.excerpt: A small office building in Palo Alto could give way to a multifamily building not far from one of the city’s Caltrain stops.  San Mateo-based nonprofit Minority Television Project proposed the plan to erect a six-story building at 555 College Avenue with 70 apartments and roughly 8,000 square feet of ground-floor commercial space, Mercury News reported. The nonprofit purchased the property in 2023 for $7.1 million, according to documents filed with the Santa Clara County Recorder’s Office.  The building would consist of five stories of housing above a ground floor that could accommodate retail, dining or other commercial uses. The […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Minority Television Project proposes 70-unit, 6-story apartment at 555 College Ave, Palo Alto, via SB 79.
Site within 0.5 miles of California Avenue Caltrain station; nonprofit purchased property for $7.1M in 2023.
Nonprofit also pursuing 24-unit office-to-residential conversion at 127 Lytton Ave, same SB 79 pathway.
stories.summary: San Mateo-based nonprofit Minority Television Project filed a proposal for 70 apartments and ~8,000 SF of ground-floor commercial on 555 College Avenue (Palo Alto) using SB 79 transit-oriented development incentives. The property, purchased for $7.1M in 2023, qualifies for taller-than-usual zoning within 0.5 miles of California Avenue Caltrain. The nonprofit is also seeking SB 79 approval for a 24-unit office-to-residential conversion at 127 Lytton Avenue, targeting the same Caltrain station.
stories.impact: Major supply signal for Bay Area: SB 79 (effective immediately) is unlocking multifamily density near Caltrain stations in Palo Alto—a historically entitlement-constrained market. Minority Television Project's dual conversions (office-to-residential + new construction) suggest aggressive use of SB 79 to bypass restrictive local zoning. As AI boom drives tech worker immigration to Silicon Valley, SB 79-enabled apartments near transit hubs will absorb demand. Developers should identify other office buildings within SB 79 corridors in Palo Alto, Mountain View, and Sunnyvale; the statute creates a 2-3 year window for conversions before local zoning reasserts itself.

stories.title: Steel IQ’s 3D-printed steel could slash builds from months to weeks
stories.excerpt: The new Lake Dallas startup Steel IQ believes that its 3D digital extrusion and printing is the sustainable future for affordable housing in America.  One of the major problems in the United States’ development pipeline is a lack of skilled construction workers. Steel IQ’s 3D printed steel frames may cost more to install, but require fewer skilled workers and cut down on framing timelines. The process uses a  digital model of a building, finds its dimensions with AI assistance and prints it in steel, down to the holes for the screws, according to the Dallas Morning News. Steel IQ’s founder […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Steel IQ (Lake Dallas startup) develops 3D-printed steel framing; reduces construction timeline from months to weeks.
Process cuts labor needs, waste, cost vs. wood; uses digital model + AI for precision; zero-tree environmental benefit.
Founder Nejeeb Khan cites skilled labor shortage as key problem; used in $15M Infinity mansion (Frisco).
stories.summary: Lake Dallas-based Steel IQ is pioneering 3D-printed steel framing systems that dramatically accelerate construction timelines (months to weeks) and reduce labor requirements compared to traditional wood framing. The AI-guided digital extrusion process prints structural steel with pre-cut holes, allowing on-site assembly akin to Lego blocks. While steel costs more than wood, the process offsets expenses via reduced waste, shorter schedules, and lower labor intensity. The system has already been deployed on high-end residential (e.g., $15M Infinity mansion in Frisco).
stories.impact: Structural cost-reduction opportunity for multifamily: Steel IQ's technology could materially improve project feasibility for labor-constrained, high-velocity builds, especially in Texas and other skilled-labor-short markets. If 3D steel framing can reduce construction duration by 30–50% and lower total labor spend, developer IRRs improve and pro-forma sensitivity to interest-rate delays weakens. Track Steel IQ's adoption rate and cost curve; if scalable to mid-rise multifamily (5–10 story), it could unlock affordable-housing feasibility in supply-constrained submarkets by reducing per-unit hard costs by 5–10%.
